speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. You were about to tell me about the Danube River. But before we get into that, I want to ask you. You mentioned that your wife was with you in Italy. How were you able to keep in contact with your wife, and how frequently could you see her? It was a secret marriage, and you were traveling quite a bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the marriage ceremony, we left the building, and we're not walking together, she with her father, and I with my brother. And I had hardly any chance to be with her alone. We were very, very careful to keep it secret. And when I was in Italy, I don't remember anymore about [INAUDIBLE]. But I spoke to her by telephone from Italy. And she came. She had no trouble with traveling. She has also a passport. There was not then ways of becoming a German passport. But it was a good passport. She came to visit me in Fiume. And since it was my birthday, my birthday was on the 21st of September, it was a very happy birthday. Because on the 19th or 20th, the [? driver ?] had left, and the day afterwards Lore was here. And I spent two days with her in Fiume until I proceeded [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to Athens. We made arrangements, the final arrangements with [INAUDIBLE]. I'd already spoken with the representatives. All the arrangements-- all the arrangements were made. These people, you don't make anything in writing. This is how you conclude it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about the Danube.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why that was a good route.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we did not need the transit visas. We could-- in theory, we could leave Vienna, the boats left Vienna. And from everywhere that people came to Vienna. So from Slovakia, from Poland, because this was where the Action started. And since you could go down the Danube on one of these river boats, with shovels, these wheels, down the Danube to where the Danube meets the Black Sea. Or there's a [INAUDIBLE]. But the Danube was wide enough [INAUDIBLE] to gather the fairly big ships could get together, only with one or two smaller ports in between. And there they were to be transferred. So there was no problem. But the British diplomatic service intervened in these countries. So they said, these people are traveling with false papers. They are committing a crime. If somebody commits a crime, according to point so and so, and so and so, he can be taken off a ship, even although the Danube is international. Of course, the sense of this, they can't risk, of course, trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the word Action.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I would want to know why you are using that word as you describe what your work was doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We called it the Action. We called it FRP, the Action FRP.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So that was part of the title?

speaker: interviewee
text: FRP, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned in your book--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- when we spoke about it to others, we spoke of Action, the Action, the Action.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned in your book that you were fighting what you call a four-front war. Can you explain the four fronts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, British authorities, whose blockade we had to break, then the Nazis who could arrest us any moment, and kill us whenever they felt like it, it was a British establishment. We tried to-- but it was the Jewish establishment which tried to prevent them. Because they thought the British would get angry with us, and therefore that we will not be on good terms with the British after the war, when in the event of division of Palestine will come up. They didn't understand that in politics, friendship doesn't count, but power, what power you have. That counts. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the fourth?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the fourth was the forces of nature, the storms on the seas with sea under the ships, sickness, thirst, and hunger on the ships.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you about some of the-- there must have been dozens of details that you needed to take care of just equipping the ships.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me an idea how you were able to handle things like food and medical supplies, and how did you provide place for everybody to sleep? What were the conditions when they got stuck on a long passage? Can you tell me a little bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Firstly the the left us in [INAUDIBLE]. We wanted to get out. There was enough space to lie down, and lie here. There were no special accommodations. It was the Then a few days before the Crystal night, which was formally called Kristallnacht. It's a German word. Because the crystal of the stores which was broken was still lying in the street. It should be called The German National Program. That's what it was. With, we ourselves, fell for the trick of [INAUDIBLE] calling it the Kristallnacht. But a few-- it was the 9th of November, a few days before, I believe on the 7th, but not earlier than the 6th of November, those would be on the train in [? Arnostadt. ?] [? Arnostadt ?] was the border station between Austria and Italy, where we had been held up for so long. Where all the new ships, these were to come. With the [? Davaris ?] and again, because it's a [INAUDIBLE] and a new ship, an old ship, but new in our service. And I believe on the two transports there were some 1,100 people, more than 1,100-odd people, who left Vienna just before this National Program, the so-called Kristallnacht. And taken through guards, through guards. And guards, of course, you must know that Romania was a country which tries to live on bribery. The government didn't pay the employees. They had to get their money from bribery. When I arrived from Athens, my ship, in Constanta, I was one of the first ones out. And there was absolutely kosher. I didn't smuggle anything. And the man at the counter where we lined up, had the luggage there, asked me, do you have anything to declare. I said, no. He said, open your luggage. So I opened it. He said, take the things out that you smuggled. I say, I don't smuggle anything. So he took the things and thew them in the air, like a fountain that came down. And shouted to the other people, this is what happens if somebody claims he has nothing to declare. Yes, he wanted-- only he wanted-- so many ways for me as a briber, for bribery, where they thought I was rich. I think they wanted about $10 from me. And I couldn't give him the $10. But I thought I put myself into the wrong. I was absolutely kosher. I had nothing to declare. So the others, of course, paid him. From this I had learned my lesson in Romania. In Romania there was no intervention from the government. But the commander of the port, the police, the inspector, all wanted money. So the police inspector, I paid. I use always the same phrase. I give it to you for charity, according to your own-- I give this for charity, according to your own disposition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I give him-- I don't know anymore how much-- to the chief of police, and to bribe the port master. And there was no problem with transferring. They said you can go through, but you cannot transfer. So transferring the people from the Danube ships to the and the and It was the first trip of the As you went through and did all these different things, how did you know who to trust?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't trust anybody. But I had a confidence man. When I was in my hotel room in Galati. This is about 30 or 40 miles from Bucharest on the Danube. A man came in one morning with a fur coat, a little bit shabby, but very elegant, with fur collar and [INAUDIBLE], and a card on which was written John L. [? Popesko, ?] Special Assistant to the Chief of Immigration of the Jews, and his phone number.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what's that? He said, that's what I am. So he told me that he can-- that I'll have trouble with Romanian authorities. I do not know my way. He can arrange everything. He's known as an arranger. And I should make him-- and that's why he made himself my assistant. And he told me, I have to pay him, of course. So I did not know. I inquired in the Jewish community. Who is this John L. [? Popesko? ?] maybe he will try to take me for a ride. Though they said, he's a well-known arranger for illegal things. He's very capable. He can steal your socks without taking your shoes off. I said, this is the only man with whom I can deal? They said, yes. There's another man too. They gave me the name. I don't know the name anymore. I said, what can he do? They said, he can do the same thing. He can enter a revolving door, and will be behind, and then come out in front of you. He's just as good as [? Popesko. ?] I said, what's the difference? He said, both of them will sell you their grandmother for five [INAUDIBLE], for nothing. But [? Popesko ?] will deliver. So I stuck with [? Popesko. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? Popesko ?] made these connections with the Chief of Police, with the Chief of port. And each time he took a certain amount for himself, regardless of the amount I give, to bribe myself. I then give it to him to give them. But he was really able to take your socks off without your shoes, taking your shoes off.

speaker: interviewer
text: A moment ago you spoke about the four-front war. And you told me a little bit about what the Germans were doing to stop you, and what the Germans were helping and not helping, and what the Jewish community was doing. But can you tell me a little bit more about what the British were doing? What were the British doing diplomatically to stop you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British led against us a four-front war, a full war. They mobilized their diplomatic service to interfere with the countries through which we had to come, and which they acted to have us arrested. And we were all arrested. I was in jails in Romania, in Bulgaria, in Hungary, in Yugoslavia, and in Portugal. But I always got out somehow. And they sent the navy after us. The British, I think, in my book of the whole little flotilla of destroyers they sent to the Palestine coast. Because the police boats couldn't take care of us. The Greek smugglers were very clever. They sent the navy against us. They sent the air force against us. And the British Intelligence Services worked against us. I have documents prepared for you for all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have to use any false names in the course of your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me rephrase that. At this point in time-- because I know you used a false name later on for other work that you did. But at this point in time, did you use any false names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't use a false name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were never known by any other name that people could contact you by?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was known under the name of [? Tef, ?] and it was the name which I had in my fraternity. Everyone has a fraternity name. And under the name of [? Tef, ?] I made myself known by-- they wanted a picture of [? Tef, ?] from me. I said, I don't have a picture of [? Tef. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a letter sent to me from Yugoslavia. Supposedly from a girl, a very foolish girl, who wrote to me, this you would be happy to remember. And here is, you see this ship. And it's [? Tef-- ?] [? Tef ?] in charge of the loading.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was some guy there, [? Tef. ?] And this was [? Tef. ?] And they're looking for [? Tef. ?] They wanted to find out from me where [? Tef ?] is. But later on, I suppose they knew. But I didn't use myself the name [? Tef. ?] But I used it in order to misdirect them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war finally began in September of 1939, how did this impact your efforts to get Jews out of Europe, and into Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: It made it much more difficult. Because ships [INAUDIBLE], ships became not available. Everyone wanted to transport Jews for that. And also the Mediterranean, became later. I gave up at that time, it was war territories, it was fighting territory between the British and the Italian Navy. But most of all, because the possibility for Jews to escape, to get out of the countries where they were locked in was reduced. It was harder to get out. The countries now had all lived seemingly to keep foreigners out. There was war. They didn't know enough about you. So they didn't give you a visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started, when Germany invaded Poland, I was in Athens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any personal preparations in anticipation should a war begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. A few days before this, there was an agreement announced between [INAUDIBLE] and Stalin. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] the contract that says I'm fighting for the same thing, and so on, against the west, Britain. And it was Germany and Russia. With this, Hitler had a chief to cover himself against an attack from the East. And yet, free hand against the West interests. And so it's a question of a few weeks, or a few days that they will attack in the West. And he attacked two days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew what was coming. And I knew that it would be very difficult to come to the railroad station. I had to get out. Because Europe was divided into two parts by this, this Axis, the Axis from Germany to Italy. You had west of it, and east of it. And I was in Athens, east of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] where the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where the Jews-- where the British had forces everywhere. And the Americans had nothing there. I was in the wrong side. And I had to come. So I wanted to do anything. In order to save myself, I had to come to the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had, a few days before that, I had gone to the rescue squad. And I had bribed there, a bribe for the rescue squad, and then the war breaks out, and it will be known all over, you come immediately to the hotel at the [? Nepara. ?] That's where I stayed. It was the best hotel in town. At the [? Nepara ?] Hotel, and pick me up there, and bring me to the railroad station. As soon as I heard on the radio that the Germans had invaded Poland, I went to the cashier to pay. And there were three or four people in front of me. And I wanted what was-- why-- what else would I want?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I described it in my book. Yes. I wanted to pay. And there was the cashier. There were three or four people in front of me. But I had to do something else. I had to get the bill first, from one desk, and then to go to the cashier.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the desk to get my bill. And there were three or four people ahead of me. When I came back, there were 10 people there. I don't know with [INAUDIBLE], it might have been 8 or 12. But many people who were-- everyone fled, a panic.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the British, of course. There were many British tourists in the hotel at Athens at this time. British traveled a lot before the war. And it was agonizingly slow, the payment, and so on. I was afraid that this guy will not wait. But when I was ready, here he came in. And I told him to wait for me. And he waited for me. I paid. And I left the hotel. And we drove now to the railroad station, very fast, with a signal blowing, the emergency things all that time, blowing. I was sitting next to him. And there was a picture, which I'll never forget this. The donkeys in the street, and the carts. Everybody went to the side, and see this ambulance that drove me to railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from the [? railroad? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a ticket on the Orient Express to Paris. Which, of course due, only Italy was not in the war yet. Because Germany was bothering-- Germany was bothering Italy. But Italy was not in the war yet. Italy entered later, a year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't want to go all the way on the first train. It was too risky to get near to Germany. So I left the train in Zagreb, which is now the capital of Slovenia. I left the train in Zagreb, and took a taxi from Zagreb to Milan, a terrific distance, unbelievable. But I had to pay him in advance. I paid him advance, and I drove the whole night long to Milan. And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Were you able-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in those first few months that the war was raging, were you able to get other ships organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I got to Switzerland. I must tell you, I had a Swiss visa. How did I get a Swiss visa? I went to the British embassy, in Bucharest. And I told them I want a visa to Britain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, to Britain, you know, does he know who you are? I said, yes, but I have to get there. I'm running out of money. I need money from the rich British Jews. And they gave me a visa in which was written for visit, for tourist only.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the intention, of course, was the moment I'm not a tourist, only then arrest me and say, help me. And my intention was never to go to Britain. But with the British visa, which was a very good visa, at the other end of Europe, I could get transit visas through all the countries on the way. And this is how I got my Italian visa, my Swiss visa, because I could be sent to Britain. Britain has given me a regular visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were you able to do in Switzerland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Switzerland I met Dr. Hecht, who was the Irgun representative for Europe. And we discussed what can we do. And from the first moment, we clicked. We have remained very good friends all our lives. We clicked, we suit each other very well. He comes from a very well-to-do Swiss family. His father owned the Swiss German Danube shipping company. And much of the shipping which went down and up the Danube went through his ships. And he was a Zionist, and a religionist. And his father was very much against Zionism. And his father Hecht was about to initiate a procedure to declare him mentally sick, so that he cannot he couldn't make debts on the name of Hecht, which was a big name in-- Hecht in Switzerland at this time was like Rockefeller here. His father was a lot like Rockefeller here, but a very well-known rich man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with his name, we had many advantages with the police, with the Swiss police. And we decided, why should we have ships for 800 people? We will have-- make a trip for 2,000 people, a big ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for this, I got in touch with people whom I knew in Romania. And they told me, you can get a ship for 2,000 people. But they want 3,000 pounds. I don't remember how much. I believe it was 2,000 pounds advance payment in order to come through the port of Sulina. I was ready to take 2,000 pounds there. Robert [? Mandler ?] I had a cousin of mine who was a real go-getter, a very capable businessman. Not a Zionist, they called him a Zionist. He was only a businessman out for money. I had met him, put him in the charge of our [INAUDIBLE] in Slovakia. And he organized the transport without a ship yet. And then I told him, I have a ship, but I don't have the money. He said, Eichmann is after me. I have to send the people out. He called me. He thought we might come take him. then. Because he gets him to Romania, and the Jews in Romania were safe for the moment. But the only thing is, they have to get on the ship. So I told him, I can have a ship. But I don't have 2,000 pounds. He said, I have the 2,000 pounds for you. I was sitting down. He was only appointed two months ago. And he had 2,000 pounds. The guy had succeeded to get from the Czech puppet government under the auspices of the German, the permission to get every for every Jew who leaves Slovakia a certain amount in foreign currency. I see, I see he got very much money, of he made very much money on it. Because he must have taken from people, let us say, 100 Czech crowns, which were, [INAUDIBLE] officially 12 pounds, which he had to pay to me. And actually he got for this money much more when he sold it. But he sent it to Switzerland. And he was [INAUDIBLE] of something around [INAUDIBLE] in Switzerland. Because he was killed by the Germans. He was too smart. He stayed too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with his support, we got money for the For the it was the biggest ship ever to arrive in Palestine with illegal immigrants, so-called illegal immigrants. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was after the war. It arrived after the beginning or the war. They left Constanza-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not Constanza. They were in-- no, they were, I believe, in Constanza. They left on the 1st or 2nd of February. And you know, God was with us all the time. They had there on the ship, and the British Navy stopped the ship, and the captain said, what should I do with them? I bring them to the next consulate, British consulate, and who takes them over in Smyrna.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no. We can't have them in Smyrna. The British Navy ordered him to proceed to the next British port, which was Haifa. And he wrote it in writing. And it said the ship could--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to break now to change tape, and we'll pick it up in Haifa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
